title: Homestead Coffee Table
summary: The coffee table from the Homestead collection: splayed 10° trestle legs, a chunky 2x top, and a bottom shelf — every angle in it is the same 10° saw setting.
description:
This is the Homestead console table's shorter, wider sibling — the same splayed-leg trestle language at coffee-table height, with a bottom shelf for the stack of books that justifies the whole purchase. Like the rest of the collection, exactly one angle appears anywhere in the build: 10°, set once on the miter saw.

The cut list's three flavors of 10° cut deserve the usual respect (parallel long-point-to-short-point for the legs; NOT-parallel long-point-to-long-point for the cleats and shelf supports) — mark each part's identity on it as it comes off the saw.

The build tip that's worth stealing came from the source video: tack each leg assembly together with 2" brads and glue FIRST, then add the pocket holes and screws. The brads act as clamps holding the angled joints in alignment — angled parts squirm under a driven screw, and the tack-first method removes the squirm. Top choice is yours: the drawn 2x8s for the chunky farmhouse look, or the builder's own modification of two 1x12s for something lighter. Yellow-toned lumber indoors; red-toned if this ends up on a covered porch.

tools.note: One 10° setting for every angled cut in the table.
1-1/2" setting, 2-1/2" pocket screws — the hidden-joinery option the table deserves.
The tack-first trick: 2" brads and glue hold the angled joints while you add the real fasteners.

materials.name: 2x4, 12 ft
materials.note: The leg assemblies. Yellow-toned for indoors.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Bottom shelf and leg base plates.

materials.name: 2x8, 12 ft
materials.note: The top — or substitute two 1x12 @ 42" like the source build did, for a lighter look.

cutList.note: Or two 1x12 @ 42" — the source's own modification.
10° both ends, PARALLEL, long point to short point.
10° both ends, NOT parallel, long point to long point.
10° both ends, NOT parallel, long point to long point.
10° both ends, NOT parallel, long point to long point.

steps.title: Build two identical leg assemblies
steps.body:
Assemble each trestle end — two splayed legs, top cleat, shelf support — using the video's sequence: glue and 2" brads first to tack every angled joint in perfect alignment, THEN drill the 1-1/2" pocket holes and drive the 2-1/2" pocket screws.

Angled joints creep when a screw hits them; the brads are disposable clamps that stop it. Pocket holes go on the faces that end up hidden — down and inward.

steps.title: Attach the base plates
steps.body: Screw a 2x6 base plate across the bottom of each leg assembly with the self-tapping screws. The plates widen the table's stance and give the splayed legs their landing.

steps.title: Join the legs to the top
steps.body:
Lay the top boards face down, position the leg assemblies on the underside, and screw through the top cleats into the top.

The nicer-top option, as ever with this collection: pocket-hole the top boards into one panel first (1-1/2" holes, 2-1/2" screws), then mount it as a unit — flatter, tighter seams, no gaps collecting crumbs.

steps.title: Drop in the shelf and finish
steps.body:
Set the three 2x6 shelf boards on the shelf supports between the legs and screw up from underneath with the 2-1/2" screws.

Sand — break the top's edges generously; shins negotiate with coffee tables daily — then stain and topcoat. The collection look is a warm stain with visible grain; the durability requirement is a real topcoat, because this surface hosts mugs, feet, and board-game nights.

images.alt: Farmhouse coffee table with splayed trestle legs, thick wood top, and bottom shelf
